answer:
Latency Budget — Design Implementation

Core Definition

A latency budget for AI features allocates the total acceptable response time across all steps in the pipeline: pre-processing, retrieval, model inference, and post-processing. It ensures AI-powered features meet user experience targets (typically under 3 seconds for interactive features).

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a latency budget for the AI assistant to respond within 3 seconds total. He allocated: 200ms for PII redaction, 500ms for RAG retrieval, 2000ms for model inference, and 300ms for post-processing and de-tokenization.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// latency-budget.ts — Pipeline with timing and budget enforcement
interface PipelineTimings {
  piiRedaction: number;
  ragRetrieval: number;
  modelInference: number;
  postProcess: number;
  total: number;
}

const BUDGET = {
  piiRedaction: 200,
  ragRetrieval: 500,
  modelInference: 2000,
  postProcess: 300,
  total: 3000,
};

async function timedStep<T>(
  name: string, budget: number, fn: () => Promise<T>
): Promise<{ result: T; duration: number }> {
  const start = performance.now();
  const result = await Promise.race([
    fn(),
    new Promise<never>((_, reject) =>
      setTimeout(() => reject(new Error(`${name} exceeded ${budget}ms budget`)), budget * 1.5)
    ),
  ]);
  const duration = performance.now() - start;

  if (duration > budget) {
    console.warn(`[LATENCY] ${name}: ${duration.toFixed(0)}ms (budget: ${budget}ms)`);
  }

  return { result, duration };
}

async function aiPipeline(userMessage: string): Promise<{ response: string; timings: PipelineTimings }> {
  const t1 = await timedStep('piiRedaction', BUDGET.piiRedaction, () =>
    piiService.redact(userMessage)
  );

  const t2 = await timedStep('ragRetrieval', BUDGET.ragRetrieval, () =>
    retriever.search(t1.result)
  );

  const t3 = await timedStep('modelInference', BUDGET.modelInference, () =>
    client.messages.create({
      model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
      max_tokens: 512, // Limit output tokens to control inference time
      system: `${SYSTEM_PROMPT}\nContext: ${t2.result.join('\n')}`,
      messages: [{ role: 'user', content: t1.result }],
    }).then(r => r.content[0].text)
  );

  const t4 = await timedStep('postProcess', BUDGET.postProcess, () =>
    Promise.resolve(piiService.deTokenize(t3.result))
  );

  return {
    response: t4.result,
    timings: {
      piiRedaction: t1.duration,
      ragRetrieval: t2.duration,
      modelInference: t3.duration,
      postProcess: t4.duration,
      total: t1.duration + t2.duration + t3.duration + t4.duration,
    },
  };
}

// Streaming variant — improves perceived latency
async function* aiPipelineStreaming(userMessage: string) {
  const safe = await piiService.redact(userMessage);
  const context = await retriever.search(safe);

  const stream = client.messages.stream({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
    max_tokens: 512,
    system: `${SYSTEM_PROMPT}\nContext: ${context.join('\n')}`,
    messages: [{ role: 'user', content: safe }],
  });

  for await (const chunk of stream) {
    if (chunk.type === 'content_block_delta') {
      yield piiService.deTokenize(chunk.delta.text);
    }
  }
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

HR managers complained the AI assistant was "slow" with 4-5 second response times. Manoj added timing instrumentation and found: PII redaction 150ms, RAG retrieval 1200ms (over budget!), model inference 2500ms, post-processing 100ms — totaling 3950ms. He optimized RAG retrieval by adding an embedding cache (repeat queries hit cache in 50ms instead of 1200ms) and reduced model output to 512 max tokens. He also implemented streaming so users see the first tokens within 700ms, making the response feel instant even though full completion takes 2.5 seconds.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
RAG retrieval dominates latency budgetCache embeddings for frequent queries; pre-compute common searches
Long model output exceeds inference budgetLimit max_tokens; use streaming for perceived speed
No monitoring for latency regressionsLog timings per step; alert when P95 exceeds budget

How to Validate

  • Run pipeline with timing — verify each step within budget
  • Test with cached vs uncached queries — confirm cache hit speedup
  • Verify streaming delivers first token within 700ms
  • Load test with concurrent requests — confirm latency under load

Latency Budget — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Latency budget debugging involves identifying which pipeline step is consuming more than its allocated time, profiling the bottleneck, and optimizing or restructuring the pipeline to meet the overall latency target.

Manoj's Project Context

In ContactsHub, the AI-powered contact search had a 2-second latency target but consistently took 3.5 seconds. Manoj profiled the pipeline and found that embedding generation and vector search were running sequentially when they could be parallelized.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// BEFORE: Sequential pipeline (3500ms)
async function searchContactsSlow(query: string) {
  const embedding = await generateEmbedding(query);     // 500ms
  const vectorResults = await vectorSearch(embedding);    // 800ms
  const filtered = await applyFilters(vectorResults);     // 200ms
  const ranked = await rerankResults(query, filtered);    // 400ms
  const summary = await generateSummary(query, ranked);   // 1600ms
  return { results: ranked, summary };
}

// AFTER: Optimized pipeline (1800ms)
const embeddingCache = new Map<string, number[]>();

async function searchContactsFast(query: string) {
  // Optimization 1: Cache embeddings
  const cacheKey = query.toLowerCase().trim();
  let embedding = embeddingCache.get(cacheKey);
  if (!embedding) {
    embedding = await generateEmbedding(query);
    embeddingCache.set(cacheKey, embedding);
  }

  // Optimization 2: Parallelize independent operations
  const [vectorResults, structuredFilters] = await Promise.all([
    vectorSearch(embedding),           // 800ms
    extractFiltersFromQuery(query),     // 200ms (runs in parallel)
  ]);

  // Apply filters to vector results
  const filtered = applyStructuredFilters(vectorResults, structuredFilters);

  // Optimization 3: Skip re-ranking for simple queries
  const ranked = filtered.length > 20
    ? await rerankResults(query, filtered.slice(0, 20))  // Only re-rank top 20
    : filtered;

  // Optimization 4: Stream summary generation
  const summaryStream = streamSummary(query, ranked.slice(0, 5));

  return { results: ranked, summaryStream };
}

// Optimization 5: Precompute popular search embeddings on startup
async function warmEmbeddingCache() {
  const popularQueries = ['Mumbai contacts', 'engineering team', 'recent contacts'];
  await Promise.all(
    popularQueries.map(async q => {
      embeddingCache.set(q, await generateEmbedding(q));
    })
  );
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

ContactsHub search consistently exceeded the 2-second budget. Manoj added `performance.now()` instrumentation to each step and found: embedding generation (500ms), vector search (800ms), filter extraction (200ms), re-ranking (400ms), and LLM summary (1600ms) — all running sequentially. His optimizations: (1) Cache embeddings — repeat queries drop from 500ms to 5ms. (2) Run vector search and filter extraction in parallel with `Promise.all` — saves 200ms. (3) Limit re-ranking to top 20 results — saves 200ms. (4) Stream the LLM summary so users see results immediately while summary generates. Total: from 3500ms to ~1800ms with cache hit, well within the 2-second budget.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Sequential steps that could run in parallelIdentify independent operations and use Promise.all
Expensive embedding generation on every queryCache embeddings; pre-warm cache with popular queries
Re-ranking all results is wastefulOnly re-rank top-K candidates (20 instead of 100)

How to Validate

  • Run search pipeline with timing — verify total under 2 seconds
  • Test cache hit path — confirm near-instant embedding lookup
  • Verify parallel execution with Promise.all timing logs
  • Load test with 10 concurrent searches — confirm no degradation

Cost Control — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

AI cost control involves monitoring and optimizing the expenses of using LLM APIs, including input/output token costs, embedding generation costs, and infrastructure costs. Without controls, AI features can produce unexpectedly high bills due to verbose prompts, unnecessary API calls, or uncontrolled user usage.

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, the AI assistant's monthly API costs spiked from $50 to $400 after launch. Manoj debugged the cost spike and found that: (1) the system prompt was 2000 tokens (sent with every request), (2) long conversation histories weren't truncated, and (3) there were no per-user rate limits.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// cost-control.ts — Token budget management and rate limiting
interface UsageRecord {
  userId: string;
  inputTokens: number;
  outputTokens: number;
  cost: number;
  timestamp: Date;
}

class CostController {
  private dailyUsage = new Map<string, number>(); // userId → token count
  private readonly DAILY_LIMIT = 10_000; // tokens per user per day
  private readonly MONTHLY_BUDGET = 100; // dollars
  private monthlySpend = 0;

  // Pricing (per 1M tokens)
  private readonly PRICING = {
    'claude-sonnet-4-6': { input: 3, output: 15 },
    'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001': { input: 0.25, output: 1.25 },
  };

  async checkBudget(userId: string, estimatedTokens: number): Promise {
    // Per-user daily limit
    const userDaily = this.dailyUsage.get(userId) ?? 0;
    if (userDaily + estimatedTokens > this.DAILY_LIMIT) {
      throw new Error('Daily token limit reached. Try again tomorrow.');
    }

    // Global monthly budget
    if (this.monthlySpend > this.MONTHLY_BUDGET) {
      throw new Error('Monthly AI budget exhausted.');
    }

    return true;
  }

  recordUsage(userId: string, model: string, inputTokens: number, outputTokens: number) {
    const pricing = this.PRICING[model];
    const cost = (inputTokens * pricing.input + outputTokens * pricing.output) / 1_000_000;

    this.monthlySpend += cost;
    this.dailyUsage.set(userId, (this.dailyUsage.get(userId) ?? 0) + inputTokens + outputTokens);

    console.log(`[COST] ${model}: ${inputTokens}in + ${outputTokens}out = $${cost.toFixed(4)}`);
  }
}

// Optimized system prompt — trimmed from 2000 to 400 tokens
const OPTIMIZED_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are an HR leave assistant.
Rules: Answer from provided context only. Be concise (under 150 words).
Leave types: annual (20d), sick (10d), personal (5d). Carry-over: max 5d by Mar 31.`;
// Before: Long-winded prompt with examples and edge cases = 2000 tokens
// After: Concise rules + let RAG context provide specifics = 400 tokens

// Response caching for common questions
const responseCache = new Map<string, { response: string; expiry: number }>();

async function cachedChat(userId: string, message: string): Promise<string> {
  const key = message.toLowerCase().trim();
  const cached = responseCache.get(key);
  if (cached && Date.now() < cached.expiry) {
    return cached.response; // Zero API cost!
  }

  await costController.checkBudget(userId, 600); // Estimate

  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001', // Use cheaper model for FAQ-type questions
    max_tokens: 256, // Limit output length
    system: OPTIMIZED_SYSTEM_PROMPT,
    messages: [{ role: 'user', content: message }],
  });

  costController.recordUsage(
    userId, 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
    response.usage.input_tokens, response.usage.output_tokens
  );

  responseCache.set(key, { response: response.content[0].text, expiry: Date.now() + 3600_000 });
  return response.content[0].text;
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

After launching the HR AI assistant, the monthly bill jumped from $50 to $400. Manoj added token logging per request and found: (1) The system prompt used 2000 tokens — sent with every single API call, accounting for 40% of input tokens. He trimmed it to 400 tokens. (2) Conversation histories grew unbounded — some users had 50-message sessions costing $2 each. He capped history to 10 messages with summarization. (3) No per-user limits — one power user made 200 queries/day. He added a 10K token daily limit per user. (4) FAQs weren't cached — "How many leave days do I have?" was hitting the API 50 times/day. He added response caching with 1-hour TTL. After all fixes, monthly cost dropped to $60.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Verbose system prompts multiply cost on every requestTrim to essentials; move details to RAG context
No per-user limits allows cost abuseImplement daily token budgets per user
Repeated FAQ queries hit the API unnecessarilyCache responses with TTL for common questions

How to Validate

  • Monitor daily and monthly token usage — confirm within budgets
  • Verify system prompt token count is under 500
  • Test per-user rate limiting — confirm 429 after limit exceeded
  • Check cache hit rate — should be >30% for FAQ-heavy workloads

Cost Control — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

Two approaches to AI cost control: model routing (automatically choosing cheaper models for simpler tasks) versus caching and deduplication (avoiding redundant API calls through response caching and request batching). Both reduce costs, but through different mechanisms.

Manoj's Project Context

For ContactsHub, Manoj compared model routing (using Haiku for simple searches, Sonnet for complex reasoning) versus aggressive caching (caching search embeddings and AI summaries) to reduce the AI feature's monthly cost.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Approach A — Smart model routing
async function routeQuery(query: string): Promise<string> {
  // Classify complexity (cheap — use Haiku for classification itself)
  const classification = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001',
    max_tokens: 10,
    messages: [{
      role: 'user',
      content: `Classify this contact search query as SIMPLE or COMPLEX.
SIMPLE: name lookup, basic filter. COMPLEX: multi-criteria, reasoning needed.
Query: "${query}"
Return only: SIMPLE or COMPLEX`,
    }],
  });

  const isComplex = classification.content[0].text.includes('COMPLEX');
  const model = isComplex ? 'claude-sonnet-4-6' : 'claude-haiku-4-5-20251001';

  return await searchWithModel(query, model);
}

// Approach B — Multi-layer caching
class CachedAIService {
  private responseCache = new Map<string, { data: string; ts: number }>();
  private embeddingCache = new Map<string, number[]>();
  private readonly RESPONSE_TTL = 3600_000; // 1 hour

  async search(query: string): Promise<string> {
    const key = this.normalizeQuery(query);

    // Layer 1: Exact response cache
    const cached = this.responseCache.get(key);
    if (cached && Date.now() - cached.ts < this.RESPONSE_TTL) {
      return cached.data; // $0 cost
    }

    // Layer 2: Cached embedding (save embedding API call)
    let embedding = this.embeddingCache.get(key);
    if (!embedding) {
      embedding = await generateEmbedding(query);
      this.embeddingCache.set(key, embedding);
    }

    const results = await vectorSearch(embedding);
    const summary = await generateSummary(query, results);

    this.responseCache.set(key, { data: summary, ts: Date.now() });
    return summary;
  }

  private normalizeQuery(q: string): string {
    return q.toLowerCase().trim().replace(/\s+/g, ' ');
  }
}

// Best practice: Combine BOTH approaches
async function costOptimizedSearch(query: string): Promise<string> {
  // First: check cache (free)
  const cached = cache.get(normalizeQuery(query));
  if (cached) return cached;

  // Then: route to appropriate model (cost-efficient)
  return await routeQuery(query);
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

ContactsHub's AI search cost $200/month for 500 daily active users. Manoj analyzed query patterns and found: 60% of queries were simple name lookups, 30% were repeated common searches ("engineering team", "Mumbai office"), and only 10% were complex multi-criteria searches. He implemented both approaches: model routing sent 60% of queries to Haiku (12x cheaper), and caching eliminated 30% of API calls entirely. Combined savings: from $200/month to $45/month — a 78% reduction while maintaining quality for complex queries.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Classification itself costs tokensUse cheapest model (Haiku) for routing; amortize across saved costs
Cache returns stale results after data changesInvalidate cache on data mutations; use short TTLs
Misrouting complex queries to cheap modelDefault to better model when classification confidence is low

How to Validate

  • Track cost per query with and without routing — confirm savings
  • Monitor cache hit rate — target >25% for search workloads
  • Verify complex queries still get Sonnet-quality responses
  • Compare monthly API bill before and after optimizations

Human-in-the-loop — Design Implementation

Core Definition

Human-in-the-loop (HITL) is a design pattern where AI system outputs are reviewed, approved, or corrected by a human before being acted upon. It balances AI automation with human judgment for high-stakes decisions where errors could have significant consequences.

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a HITL workflow for the AI assistant's leave approval recommendations. The AI analyzes team availability, leave balance, and policy compliance, then recommends approve/reject — but the HR manager makes the final decision.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// hitl-approval.ts — Human-in-the-loop leave approval
interface AIRecommendation {
  decision: 'approve' | 'reject' | 'needs_review';
  confidence: number; // 0-1
  reasoning: string;
  factors: {
    balanceSufficient: boolean;
    teamCoverage: boolean;
    policyCompliant: boolean;
    conflictsDetected: string[];
  };
}

async function analyzeLeaveRequest(request: LeaveRequest): Promise<AIRecommendation> {
  const [balance, teamSchedule, policy] = await Promise.all([
    getLeaveBalance(request.employeeId),
    getTeamSchedule(request.teamId, request.startDate, request.endDate),
    getLeavePolicy(request.leaveType),
  ]);

  const response = await client.messages.create({
    model: 'claude-sonnet-4-6',
    max_tokens: 500,
    system: `You are an HR leave approval assistant. Analyze the request and provide a recommendation.
Be conservative: when in doubt, recommend 'needs_review' rather than auto-approving.`,
    messages: [{
      role: 'user',
      content: `Leave request:
- Employee: ${request.employeeName} (${request.employeeId})
- Type: ${request.leaveType}
- Dates: ${request.startDate} to ${request.endDate}
- Balance remaining: ${balance.remaining} days
- Team members on leave during this period: ${teamSchedule.onLeave.join(', ') || 'none'}
- Policy: ${JSON.stringify(policy)}

Return JSON: { decision, confidence (0-1), reasoning, factors: { balanceSufficient, teamCoverage, policyCompliant, conflictsDetected } }`,
    }],
  });

  return JSON.parse(response.content[0].text);
}

// Route based on confidence
async function processLeaveRequest(request: LeaveRequest) {
  const recommendation = await analyzeLeaveRequest(request);

  if (recommendation.confidence > 0.9 && recommendation.decision === 'approve') {
    // High confidence: auto-queue (still visible to HR manager)
    await queueForApproval(request, recommendation, 'auto-queued');
    await notifyManager(request.managerId, 'Auto-recommended for approval', recommendation);
  } else if (recommendation.confidence < 0.6 || recommendation.decision === 'needs_review') {
    // Low confidence: flag for manual review
    await flagForManualReview(request, recommendation);
    await notifyManager(request.managerId, 'Needs your manual review', recommendation);
  } else {
    // Medium confidence: show recommendation, let HR decide
    await queueForApproval(request, recommendation, 'ai-recommended');
    await notifyManager(request.managerId, `AI recommends: ${recommendation.decision}`, recommendation);
  }
}

// Feedback loop: log HR overrides for model improvement
async function recordDecision(requestId: string, hrDecision: string, aiDecision: string) {
  await db.feedbackLog.create({
    data: { requestId, hrDecision, aiDecision, overridden: hrDecision !== aiDecision },
  });
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Initially, the AI auto-approved all leave requests with sufficient balance, without checking team coverage. An entire 4-person team got approved for the same week, leaving no coverage. Manoj redesigned the system with confidence-based routing: the AI now checks balance, team schedule conflicts, and policy compliance. When confidence is below 90%, the HR manager reviews the recommendation with full context. He added a feedback loop that logs every HR override — after one month, the data showed the AI was overridden 15% of the time, primarily on team-coverage decisions. This feedback improved future recommendations.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
AI auto-approves without sufficient checksDefault to needs_review; only auto-queue at >90% confidence
HR managers rubber-stamp AI recommendationsHighlight conflicts and risks prominently in the review UI
No learning from HR overridesLog overrides and review monthly to improve AI prompt/logic

How to Validate

  • Submit overlapping team leave requests — verify AI flags conflict
  • Verify low-confidence requests route to manual review
  • Check feedback log captures HR overrides with reasons
  • Measure AI vs HR agreement rate — target >85%

Human-in-the-loop — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

HITL debugging involves diagnosing issues where the human review process breaks down — reviewers are overwhelmed with too many requests, the AI confidence scores are miscalibrated, or the feedback loop isn't improving model performance over time.

Manoj's Project Context

In the HR Leave Management System, the HITL workflow had a problem: 80% of requests were routed to manual review because the AI's confidence scores were too conservative. HR managers were overwhelmed, defeating the purpose of AI assistance.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// BEFORE: Conservative thresholds — too many manual reviews
const THRESHOLDS = {
  autoApprove: 0.95,  // Almost nothing reaches 95% confidence
  manualReview: 0.70, // Everything below 95% goes to manual
};
// Result: 80% of requests go to manual review

// AFTER: Calibrated thresholds based on historical data
async function calibrateThresholds(): Promise<{ autoApprove: number; escalate: number }> {
  // Analyze past 30 days of decisions
  const history = await db.feedbackLog.findMany({
    where: { createdAt: { gte: thirtyDaysAgo } },
  });

  // Find confidence threshold where HR agrees with AI >90% of the time
  const sorted = history.sort((a, b) => b.aiConfidence - a.aiConfidence);
  let autoApproveThreshold = 0.95;

  for (const threshold of [0.85, 0.80, 0.75, 0.70]) {
    const aboveThreshold = sorted.filter(h => h.aiConfidence >= threshold);
    const agreementRate = aboveThreshold.filter(h => !h.overridden).length / aboveThreshold.length;

    if (agreementRate >= 0.90) {
      autoApproveThreshold = threshold;
      console.log(`Calibrated auto-approve threshold: ${threshold} (${(agreementRate*100).toFixed(1)}% agreement)`);
      break;
    }
  }

  return { autoApprove: autoApproveThreshold, escalate: autoApproveThreshold - 0.2 };
}

// Priority-based review queue
interface ReviewItem {
  request: LeaveRequest;
  recommendation: AIRecommendation;
  priority: 'urgent' | 'normal' | 'low';
  reason: string;
}

function categorizeReview(rec: AIRecommendation): ReviewItem['priority'] {
  if (rec.factors.conflictsDetected.length > 0) return 'urgent';
  if (!rec.factors.teamCoverage) return 'urgent';
  if (!rec.factors.policyCompliant) return 'urgent';
  if (rec.confidence < 0.5) return 'normal';
  return 'low';
}

// Dashboard for HR managers — prioritized queue
async function getReviewQueue(managerId: string): Promise<ReviewItem[]> {
  const pending = await db.leaveRequests.findMany({
    where: { managerId, status: 'pending_review' },
    include: { aiRecommendation: true },
  });

  return pending
    .map(p => ({
      request: p,
      recommendation: p.aiRecommendation,
      priority: categorizeReview(p.aiRecommendation),
      reason: p.aiRecommendation.reasoning,
    }))
    .sort((a, b) => {
      const order = { urgent: 0, normal: 1, low: 2 };
      return order[a.priority] - order[b.priority];
    });
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

HR managers complained they were drowning in review requests — 40 per day instead of the expected 10. Manoj analyzed the data: the auto-approve threshold of 0.95 was too strict — only 20% of requests reached that confidence level, even when the AI's recommendation was correct. He ran a calibration analysis on 30 days of historical decisions and found that at a 0.80 confidence threshold, the AI agreed with HR 92% of the time. He lowered the auto-approve threshold to 0.80 and added a priority-based review queue: urgent (team conflicts), normal (policy edge cases), and low (minor questions). After recalibration, manual reviews dropped from 80% to 25%, and HR managers could focus on genuinely complex cases.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Confidence thresholds not calibrated to real dataRegularly recalibrate using historical agreement rates
All manual reviews treated equallyCategorize by priority — urgent conflicts first
Reviewers overwhelmed → rubber-stampingReduce review volume; highlight only critical decisions

How to Validate

  • Check manual review rate — target under 30% of requests
  • Verify agreement rate between AI and HR on auto-routed requests (>90%)
  • Confirm urgent items appear at top of review queue
  • Monitor override rate monthly — should decrease over time
